item.content:encoded: Man, it's weird looking back on this nearly eight years later. Had you told me then that this would be the starting point for not only a new GoI, but also one of the largest canons on the site by content, I don't know if I would have believed you.

item.title: Replaced the SCP-1360-1 Image
item.content:encoded:
Alright, so the image for SCP-1360-1 was not, in fact, compliant with the site's imaging policy, so it has subsequently been removed. I'll be on a lookout for a replacement, but if anyone here has a picture of black kevlar fabric they wouldn't mind donating, that would be groovy tunes.

Edit: So Rounderhouse is fantastic and deserves all the friends and money, because they provided a replacement before the dust even settled on the last image being removed. The update has been made.

Source of the image is HERE, and has been edited by Rounderhouse and released under cc by 3.0, etc.

Additionally, to clarify on the image of SCP-1360 proper, that was an original composition by djkaktus and, being used here with his permission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you enjoyed the skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice SCP with a sad story, and the origin of a GoI.
+1, I liked it.

Hey hey. 200+

First time breaking two centuries. Now everyone will love me!

Glad to see this skip, and the GoI it spawned became the success that they did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article had gotten over 100 upvotes prior to the Confinement episode being released, and SCP-1360's character is more than expanded upon in tales like the Peregrine series, Hector and Jacob, SCP-3560, and even Decisions We Make.

Changing this article to match an adaptation doesn't seem necessary, to be frank.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed, except Confinement Ep 3 managed to make this character very interesting. Like, I even wanna say the events of that episode should be added to this article, if that could make this article better to some people.

On the other hand, it IS a Youtube animation. Hmm, if people disagree, I really can't argue…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2632 as well.

This may not be quite enough for your tastes, but it's certainly not flat as a character, nor is it too 'ordinary' to invalidate it's inclusion. It's got both anomalousness and narrative in spades, it's just quite reserved about the whole thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not entirely sure that is how the SCP Foundation works. To that logic, I could post an article about a human with telekinetic powers. It's currently not possible for such a thing to exist. Does that make it an original and good SCP article?

As SCP-2241 demonstrates, it's all about the narrative. The anomaly is only a framing device.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is not currently possible for such a thing to exist, therefore it is anomalous.

I'm not entirely sure that is how the SCP Foundation works. To that logic, I could post an article about a human with telekinetic powers. It's currently not possible for such a thing to exist. Does that make it an original and good SCP article?

No, it doesn't. I'd have to write something very impressive to squeeze that concept past on the mainsite. Theres tons of fictional humans with telekinetic powers, so there wouldn't be much to separate it. I'd have to have some really good reasoning and deep lore.

So why does this work then, exactly? Characterization. Most of it subtle and indirect, but it's there nonetheless.

I agree there is some character to it. The machine wanting to return home to its owner isn't the best way to build character/motivation, but it's certainly not the worst. My main problem lies with the robot itself. It's just so plain. A humanoid robot who can fight, there's plenty of those. Why not have it fulfill a specific purpose? Like a war machine/guard, wanting to return home to protect its masters feeling as if it failed them, or a rescue machine feeling it needs to urgently save someone or see if they're okay. The conflict with #31 does make sense, its a robot that wants to go home to be repaired, but it's just not as emotionally grasping or dramatic as it could be to me.

Then again, this is just me. I'm not saying this piece is bad, if any of you are thinking that. It's just not as unique as it could be. Maybe I'm just not as analytical as you are, but this is just my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In addition to what others have said, I'll point out that you are correct to call this the stuff of vaguely plausible science fiction- and there lies the rub.

The simple fact is that the computing power of some of the greatest room-filling supercomputers pales in comparison to the brain of the average mouse. The requisite technology to create a self-contained sapient android with human level intelligence simply does not exist at this time, and is not likely to exist in the next few decades. It is not currently possible for such a thing to exist, therefore it is anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do understand the want for more…originality(?)…unique traits(?)…whatever - to have an anomaly stand out from the pack and be different in its own way. The gist of your original comment seemed to be your taking an issue with the lack of, I don't know, complexity or blatant 'anomalousness' of the core subject. While, sure, this could be something to make an uninteresting object really pop, not very thing needs traits to be stapled on, and in fact, plenty of things work without excessive traits just so long as there is (despite your claims to the contrary) something to draw the reader in.

So why does this work then, exactly? Characterization. Most of it subtle and indirect, but it's there nonetheless. Even without an interview, we're given insight into its goals and interests, we know what it wants, what makes it tick. What makes this work for me is that it's not just a Sci-FI robot, but a goddamn good presentation of one.

We're given just enough to see that they're broken, incomplete. There's an integral part of them that is lost, which causes them pain. Possibly as a result of this, they lose the family that they were literally made for, so they're then discarded…despite this they still fight, they have a reason to continue on…and the final nail comes down. They're abandoned by their creator, a final betrayal that takes away their will to live.

In short, its a harrowing tragedy of one character's suffering. All told in a roundabout manner that maintains credulity as an official document. This is actually a shining example of SCP writing - it tells a story without overplaying it's hand. I hope this gives you some insight as to why this is as successful as it is, and why it works for most.

A lot of people can lean from this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
there is literally nothing else that sets this apart from something like Sonny from i, Robot or any other science fiction android.

As far as I know most of those other robots don't have a self-repairing aramid covering that breaks the second law of thermodynamics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, I don't see how silly that sounds.
If you take away the fact its made by Anderson Robotics, there is literally nothing else that sets this apart from something like Sonny from i, Robot or any other science fiction android.

What you said about the GOC does not relate in anyway to what I was talking about. The GOC is an entire GOI. This is an SCP Article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"When you take away the GOC association they're just a UN peacekeeping force."

Do you see how silly that sounds? This article more or less established Anderson Robotics, and set the tone for all future references to that GOI. It doesn't matter if it's just a robot; plenty of things are "just a __". It has a compelling narrative and set in motion one of the most interesting groups of interest on the wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a robot. Thats all I find interesting about this. When you take away the Anderson Robotics GOI association, theres nothing to it other than it being an ordinary science fiction robot. Something interesting you could have done would be mentioning how its powered. Does it use an anomalous crystal? A small scale nuclear reactor?

There just isn't much detail in this to make it capture the reader. Nothing besides the fact its made by Anderson Robotics makes this stand out from any other Sci-Fi robot. I won't vote on this. It isn't poorly written enough for you to get a downvote, but it's not interesting enough for you to get an upvote either.

item.title: As Seen in Confinement...
item.content:encoded:
Episode 3 of the excellent and very funny animated series "Confinement" has dropped, and a certain android takes a major role.

Check it out HERE.

item.title: TheVolgun SCP Reading
item.content:encoded:
For those of you interested, TheVolgun has done a reading of SCP-1360.

You can find it HERE.

Not gonna lie, its pretty cool!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tis the saddest of robots.

Glad you enjoyed the article.

EDIT: Also, +150! Hot Damn!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aww… I feel bad for it.
Poor 1360.

Anyways, it was a good read. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tales go with this article nicely, and the article is also not bad.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you liked them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd rather the SCP-1360-1 angle be played more in the main article, but the tales secured my upvote nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I love the narrative flow of this scip. Yes, while it moves a bit slow to get through the full description of the 'droid, it reminds me of Stephen King's early work where he would reveal a slow trickle of information over a series of paragraphs, thus forcing the reader to put it all together for his/herself.

The singular criticism I can offer is that Addendum-1360-B could be expanded on a wee bit further to give, and forgive my use of the term, a tl;dr of the escape attempt itself.

+1

item.title: Re: Non-mod post
item.content:encoded: Tis done.

item.title: Non-mod post
item.content:encoded: Guys, these are spoilers too. Shouldn't these be preferably collapsed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Spoilers Ahoy
- I have been spoiled!

This article is supposed to be connected to the tales of the Peregrine series, since it basically serves as the basis for everything that occurs in that series. That being said, I've already stated once that the reason I never put neutralized on this article is that I always considered that a spoiler for the content of the tales.

If you need an in universe reason for why this article is not listed as neutralized a simple one would be that this is the version of the article that existed after November 17th, 2009 but before March 18th, 2014 (when Building Up and Tearing Down take place).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the actual SCP article supposed to be tally with the tales based in it? I mean, I kinda linked them up together and really hope that it will be classified as neutralized for now…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tweaking makes perfect! (most of the time) ;>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad to take it.

Goodness knows that I have tweaked this article enough to get it to a point to where it will receive such heartfelt compliments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I totally feel sorry for 1360.

My first reaction after reading the article: 'It's magnificent'.

Why?
Because. Because everything. The basic idea, the writing, the style, what is said, and above all what is not said.

IMO it remains Euclid because right now it does not want to breach containment. But who says it might not change its mind any time?

Have my +1, Mr Conwell, with heartfelt compliments!

item.title: Aw.
item.content:encoded: That last note is really well-handled. If you had dwelled on it, it could have come off as maudlin or emotionally manipulative, but by trusting your readers to get it, it ends up very sad and lonely and real. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It could be.

However, the article is largely independent of the tales with the classification designed to reflect that. In addition, I considered that to be a bit of a spoiler for the tale arc, and decided to leave it Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great skip, loved the supplements, only issue, shouldn't it be reclassed neutralized??

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: those Images are very nice indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have added images.

The first one was provided to me by djkaktus, so credits for the image goes to him.

The second image is from the following source.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So here's this fully sapient android that was meant to be a bodyguard for a little girl. In some other, happier universe, they may have become friends, and he could watch her grow up, and support and protect her through her life.

But this isn't a happy universe. So the girl's father order to take the voice from the android, because he doesn't want his guards talking.

And then the Foundation grabs him and subjects him to all kinds of tests and sample collections.

And then his Creator basically say "Sorry bud, we don't really give a fuck about you as a sapient being, it's cheaper to just make another one than try to get you from there."

And he just gives up and stops all attempts to escape. Just because he's been told to.

+1. I really like this story, and the way it's presented.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked. Dammit, I would have liked the original article. However, it feels improved, and far more interesting.

Also, I almost feel sorry for 1360. Don't know why. : /

item.title: Major Rewrite
item.content:encoded:
Alright guys.

A major rewrite of SCP-1360 has replaced the original version. Special thanks to Crayne, Jekeled and Accelerando for there help with the new version.

Hopefully you still think this is worthy of printing off Dr. Berggren.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the subject of your autopsy suggestion: The foundation is constantly skinning 1360-1, screaming pain (or the closest thing to that since 1360-1 is incapable of direct speech) and all, whenever they harvest 1360-2 for study. Since 1360-2 is of rather high value to the Foundation due to its properties I don't think they would be to keen on cracking 1360-1 open, else they damage it further and lose their source of 1360-2.

As for why the creator would allow 1360-1 to remain in Foundation hands, you could say that they could have determined at it is irretrievable at this point. Since there is no current means of 1360 to really link back to them while in custody, there is no real danger present. Trying to get 1360 out at this point would be like trying to break something out of a safety deposit box, but rather than robbing a bank, you're breaking into Site 19 and pissing off one of the most powerful organizations in the world. If anything Anderson would be more worried of the Foundation tracking 1360-1 to him following release.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your wish has been granted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not often, but I like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How often are you printing these things out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally I don't like how it takes so long in the description to get the part where the robot is actually animate/sentient. Maybe you were going for a shock/surprise there, but it just comes off as poor writing flow. The concept is 'meh' for now but there is potential if you add a creepy/weird 'hook.' Perhaps the Foundation has performed autopsies on this thing, screaming and pain and all, because they knew it would 'heal.' Maybe they found a transmitter or somehow determined that the creator wants to KEEP this thing in captivity. Maybe its been recording and transmitting on a psychic frequency all along, I don't know, but it needs some more meat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Might you take the interview out of the collapsible? It breaks flow and makes printing difficult.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad to hear. Been meaning to post this edit sooner, but alas. Things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Comparing the new interview log to the old one, it has certainly been improved in tone.
I like this SCP enough to give it a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The reasoning for the value behind the SCPs skin is explained in the text, I guess it is just buried in the scientific jargon which I've added onto. As it is described, the fibers have a tensile strength of 4000 MPa and a specific gravity of 1.1.

This makes it both stronger and lighter than Kevlar. It also is self repairing. I could foresee several applications for such a fiber if the Foundation could succeed in finding a means of preserving it once removed from the SCP's body.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For me this is a adequately rounded article, but seems to be missing a hook, r any real meat to it. If there was something to give some theorized identity to Mr. Anderson, or even a background on where this SCP was obtained, I feel that this article could be much more powerful. As it is everything seems kind of bland, we are not even given a justification for the sampling of the SCP's skin that warrants putting it through pain. Needless torture does not seem like the Foundation's forte. If it can be used as artificial skin grafts, or is bulletproof, or makes a super fuel or -something- then there would be a reason. The way things stand I have to no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would like to see this article spiced up a bit. Right now it is just "damaged robot butler who wants to get out."

I upvoted, but the following line I think needs to be changed:

Due to the subject’s ability to feel pain despite the lack of an apparent nervous system, SCP-1360-1 views itself as an incomplete model in need of repair and seeks escape to obtain what it calls its “final component.”

I'm not sure they would just take the thing's word that it feels pain just because it says it does. I would imagine them being more skeptic and testing how this thing feels "pain" without a nervous system. My hypothesis would be that it doesn't really feel pain, but that it gets sensory feedback that it is incomplete and believes this to be pain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Superbutler with a purpose?

I kinda like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a decently written, boring article.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Please take the time to read, rate and comment this article, it should be given more attention

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it. I however did feel that the mentioning of the SCP's capabilities of only writing instead of speech should have been stated earlier. I got kinda confused when the subject was "talking" until you wrote that he only expressed his thoughts through the written language.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neutral vote. Well presented, but doesn't grab me or make me wonder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Indeed. Thank you for pointing that out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hit the nail on the head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm thinking you mean "subsequent" rather than "successive"? Also, to the author, "highly obsessed" is kind of redundant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: From what I got, the first O5 was worried that excessive sample collecting would end up permanently damaging or destroying 1360-1. The other O5 was basically like "Well just don't do it THAT much until the stuff regenerates." That more or less right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for pointing those errors out, consider it fixed.

As for your question, clearly my definition was not as concise as I thought it was. SCP-1360-2 is not the mannequin's clothing, but more like a fabric skin covering its plastic casing. While the limited lifespan of the material is a set back, there is no need to halt all collection when the damage does fully repair itself. Increasing the lifespan of the material would only mean that less of it would need to be taken during collection.
